subject: ap-psychology
title: Social Psychology — Social Facilitation and Performance (EBQ)

parts.prompt: Propose a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that responds to the question.
parts.scoring_notes: A claim that meets the criteria can be awarded the point regardless of whether the responses in Parts B and C successfully support the claim.

scoring_points.description: Proposes a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that is relevant to the question about whether the presence of others influences performance.

required_elements: The presence of others makes a difference in whether someone performs well on a specific task or set of tasks.
alternatives.correct_example: The presence of others makes a difference in whether someone performs well on a specific task or set of tasks.

required_elements: When someone else is present during a performance, it is more likely that the participant will perform well.
alternatives.correct_example: When someone else is present during a performance, it is more likely that the participant will perform well.

required_elements: The presence of others will make them do worse at what they are doing.
alternatives.correct_example: The presence of others will make them do worse at what they are doing.

required_elements: People should have an audience for tasks they are good at.
alternatives.correct_example: People should have an audience for tasks they are good at.

wrong_examples: Does the presence of others influence performance? [Restates the question rather than making a claim.]
Using animals in research can tell us a lot about ourselves. [Not a claim about performance and the presence of others.]
Studying the factors that influence performance is important to social psychologists. [Not a defensible claim.]
common_traps: Restating the question instead of proposing a claim
Proposing a claim unrelated to presence of others and performance

parts.prompt: Support your claim using at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence from one of the sources.
parts.scoring_notes: Responses that use a correct citation style other than the methods prescribed by the question can earn this point for citing the source. Accurate evidence refers to the accuracy of the general pattern of the evidence cited. Since citing specific numbers is not required to score the point, slight errors in specific values do not prevent earning the point.

scoring_points.description: Uses one piece of correctly cited (using parenthetical or embedded citation), specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from one of the provided sources to support the claim.

required_elements: The results of the bar graph (Source 1) show a longer amount of time for participants to complete a new and difficult task in front of an audience than when they were alone.
alternatives.correct_example: The results of the bar graph (Source 1) show a longer amount of time for participants to complete a new and difficult task in front of an audience than when they were alone.

required_elements: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.

required_elements: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.

required_elements: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker than those who were alone.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker than those who were alone.

wrong_examples: The presence of others impacts what we do. [No evidence cited.]
The baboon study shows that the presence of other baboons made the baboons do worse on the task that required more cognitive control. [No citation.]
In Source 2, researchers used rewards to change the baboon's behavior. [Evidence not relevant to the claim about presence of others and performance.]
common_traps: Citing evidence without a proper source citation
Using evidence that is not relevant to the claim
Providing a vague or general reference that is not specific enough

parts.prompt: Explain how the evidence from Part B supports your claim using a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology.
parts.scoring_notes: The explanation and application points can be earned even if the response did not earn the point in Part B due to incorrectly cited and/or non-specific evidence. The psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding must be explicitly identified in the AP Psychology Course and Exam Description (2024). A concept that appears in the source materials themselves may not earn the application point.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Explanation): Accurately explains how the evidence from Part B relates to the claim — describes the connection between the specific evidence cited and the overall claim about presence of others and performance.

required_elements: Explains the relationship between the evidence cited and the claim about presence of others affecting performance.
alternatives.correct_example: The data from Source 1 show that people performed faster on a well-learned task in front of an audience, which supports the claim that the presence of others can improve performance on mastered skills.

wrong_examples: Simply restating the evidence without explaining how it supports the claim.
common_traps: Describing the evidence again without explaining the connection to the claim
Providing an explanation that does not reference the evidence from Part B

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Application): Additionally identifies and correctly applies a specific CED-named psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding to explain why the evidence supports the claim. The concept must go beyond what is explicitly described in the source materials.

required_elements: Correctly names and applies a CED psychological concept (e.g., social facilitation, arousal theory, drive theory, Zajonc's research) to explain the connection between the evidence and the claim.
alternatives.correct_example: This supports social facilitation theory, which predicts that the presence of others increases arousal and enhances performance on well-learned tasks — consistent with what Source 1 found for the well-practiced task condition.

wrong_examples: Using a concept that is described in the source materials themselves (e.g., if Source 1 defines social facilitation, citing social facilitation earns no application point).
Naming a concept without explaining how it connects to the evidence and claim.
common_traps: Applying a concept mentioned or defined within the sources — this does NOT earn the application point
Naming the concept but failing to explain how it connects to both the evidence and the claim

parts.prompt: Support your claim using an additional piece of specific and relevant evidence from a different source than the one used in Part B.
parts.scoring_notes: Responses that use a correct citation style other than the methods prescribed by the question can earn this point. The evidence and application points for Part E can be earned even if the response uses the same source in both Parts B and D, provided that the evidence used in Part B and Part D is different.

scoring_points.description: Uses a different piece of correctly cited, specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from a different source than the one used in Part B to support the claim.

required_elements: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.

required_elements: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.

required_elements: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker when an audience was present.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker when an audience was present.

wrong_examples: The presence of others impacts what we do. [No evidence or citation.]
In Source 2, researchers used rewards to change the baboon's behavior. [Evidence not relevant to the claim.]
Using evidence from the same source already cited in Part B. [Must be a different source.]
common_traps: Using the same source as Part B
Using evidence irrelevant to the claim

parts.prompt: Explain how the evidence from Part D supports your claim using a different psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology than the one used in Part C.
parts.scoring_notes: The explanation and application points can be earned even if the response did not earn the point in Part D due to incorrectly cited and/or non-specific evidence. The explanation and application points can be earned if the response uses the same source in both Parts B and D, provided the evidence used is different. The concept used in Part E must be different from the concept used in Part C.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Explanation): Accurately explains how the evidence from Part D relates to the claim — describes the connection between the specific evidence cited and the overall claim about presence of others and performance.

required_elements: Explains the relationship between the evidence cited in Part D and the claim about presence of others affecting performance.
alternatives.correct_example: The Source 3 data showing performance improved when observed by an evaluator supports the claim that the presence of others changes how people perform.

wrong_examples: Simply restating the evidence without explaining how it supports the claim.
common_traps: Providing the same explanation as Part C
Describing the evidence again without explaining the connection

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Application): Additionally identifies and correctly applies a specific CED-named psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding — different from the one used in Part C — to explain why the evidence supports the claim.

required_elements: Correctly names and applies a different CED psychological concept than the one used in Part C (e.g., evaluation apprehension, mere presence effect, Milgram's obedience research, social comparison theory) to connect the evidence to the claim.
alternatives.correct_example: This supports the concept of evaluation apprehension — awareness of being judged by others increases arousal and motivation, explaining the improved performance when participants were observed by an evaluator (Source 3).

wrong_examples: Using the same concept as Part C.
Using a concept described in the source materials themselves.
Naming a concept without explaining how it connects to the evidence and claim.
common_traps: Reusing the same psychological concept from Part C — must be a different concept
Applying a concept mentioned or defined within the sources
Naming the concept but failing to explain how it connects to both the evidence and the claim
